title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers

input_row.title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
input_row.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true.
input_row.rationale: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy. Impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
input_row.audit: Run the following command: get pods -A -o=jsonpath=$'{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext}\n{end}' It will produce an inventory of all the privileged use on the cluster, if any (please, refer to a sample below). Further grepping can be done to automate each specific violation detection. calico-kube-controllers-57b57c56f-jtmk4: {} << No Elevated Privileges calico-node- c4xv4: {} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} {"privileged":true} << Violates 5.2.2 dashboard-metrics-scraper-7bc864c59-2m2xw: {"seccompProfile":{"type":"RuntimeDefault"}} {"allowPrivilegeEscalation":false,"readOnlyRootFilesystem":true,"runAsGroup":2001,"ru nAsUser":1001}
input_row.remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers. Default Value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/
input_row.impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true, spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_security_privileged_containers_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Enumerate all Pods in all namespaces using the Kubernetes API (e.g., `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json`).
- For each Pod, inspect:
  - `spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged`
  - `spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.privileged`
  - `spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.privileged`
- Identify any containers (including init and ephemeral containers) where `privileged: true`.
- Pass Criteria: No containers in any Pod (including init and ephemeral containers) have `securityContext.privileged` set to `true`.
- Fail Criteria: Any container (including init or ephemeral) in any Pod has `securityContext.privileged: true`.
- Optionally, check for the presence and enforcement of Pod Security Admission (PSA) policies or PodSecurityPolicies (if legacy) that restrict privileged containers cluster-wide or per-namespace.
- Example kubectl command for direct inspection:
  ```
  kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath="{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}:{.metadata.name} {range .spec.containers[*]}{.name}:{.securityContext.privileged} {end}{range .spec.initContainers[*]}{.name}:{.securityContext.privileged} {end}{range .spec.ephemeralContainers[*]}{.name}:{.securityContext.privileged} {end}{'\n'}{end}"
  ```
- For policy enforcement, check for Pod Security Admission labels (e.g., `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`) on all namespaces, which ensures privileged containers are denied by policy.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification as Automated is correct. This control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools like `kubectl`.
- The use of Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands is appropriate for this type of check, as it allows for a comprehensive and efficient inspection of all pods across namespaces.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically correct. The approach of using `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` and inspecting the `securityContext.privileged` field is valid.
- The example `kubectl` command provided is accurate and effectively extracts the necessary information to determine if any containers are privileged.
- The optional check for Pod Security Admission labels is a good addition to ensure policy enforcement.
- The program name `k8s_pod_security_privileged_containers_restricted` is descriptive and follows a logical naming convention, though it could be slightly more specific.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved to be more descriptive, such as `k8s_pod_security_privileged_containers_check`.
- Consider including a check for the presence of PodSecurityPolicies if the cluster is using a legacy setup, as some environments might still rely on them.
- It might be beneficial to include a step to log or alert when privileged containers are found, to facilitate immediate action.
- Explore using Kubernetes SDKs for more programmatic access and integration into broader compliance tools, which could enhance automation capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate Kubernetes tools and methods. The technical details are accurate and align with best practices for Kubernetes security.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Keep as-is with a minor modification to the program name for clarity.
- Consider adding additional logging or alerting mechanisms for when privileged containers are detected, to improve response times and compliance tracking.
